title: Why Sampling Takes Many Steps
concept: Because each step only removes a small amount of noise (matching how gradually the forward process added it), full generation requires many sequential passes.

exercises.prompt: When you slowly turn up a dimmer light switch, do you usually see the room reach full brightness in one click or after many small clicks?

options.text: After many small clicks, little by little
The light never changes with clicks

exercises.explain: Right! Dimmers change brightness gradually, one small step at a time — just like AI image generation removes noise step by step.

cards.title: Why Not Just One Step?
cards.body: You'd think an AI could turn static noise into a perfect picture instantly. But it actually takes dozens or even hundreds of tiny steps! Why does it move so slowly on purpose?

cards.title: Sampling in Many Steps
cards.body: Sampling means the AI creating a new image starting from pure noise. Since noise was added gradually during training (a little bit at a time), the AI also removes it gradually — a little bit at a time. Each step only cleans up a small amount, so many steps are needed to reach a clear final image.

cards.title: Cleaning a Foggy Window
cards.body: Imagine wiping a totally fogged-up window clean. One big wipe won't make it perfectly clear — you wipe a little, check, wipe a little more, check again. Each wipe removes just a bit of fog. The AI does the same thing with noise, one gentle wipe (step) at a time.

cards.title: From Static to Picture
cards.body: An AI generating a cat picture might start with 50 steps of pure random noise. Step 1 removes a tiny bit of fuzz. Step 25 shows rough shapes. Step 50 reveals a sharp, detailed cat. Skip too many steps, and the cat looks blurry or broken.

cards.title: Myth: AI Paints Instantly
cards.body: Myth: The AI just 'knows' the final image and draws it in one shot. Truth: It has no idea what the final image looks like at first — it only knows how to slightly reduce noise. The picture emerges gradually through repeated small corrections.

cards.title: One Big Jump vs. Many Small Steps
cards.body: Removing all the noise at once produces a messy, broken image. Removing it gradually across many steps produces a clean, realistic result.

left.text: Noise removed all at once → blurry, distorted, unrealistic image

right.text: Noise removed gradually → sharp, detailed, realistic image

exercises.prompt: Why does image generation (sampling) require many steps?

options.text: Because each step only removes a small amount of noise, matching how noise was added gradually
Because computers are too slow to do it in one step
Because the AI needs to ask a human for permission each step
Because images must be saved to disk after every pixel

exercises.explain: Correct! Since noise was added little by little during training, the AI reverses it little by little too, needing many steps.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The AI could just as easily remove all the noise in a single giant step and get the same quality result.
exercises.explain: False! Removing noise all at once leads to messy, low-quality images. Gradual, many-step removal produces much better results.

exercises.sentence: Each step in sampling only removes a ___ amount of noise, which is why generation takes many steps.
exercises.explain: Each step chips away just a small amount of noise, mirroring how noise was gradually added during training.

exercises.prompt: Which situation best matches how AI sampling works?

options.text: Slowly wiping fog off a window a little at a time until it's clear
Flipping a light switch on instantly
Deleting a file completely in one click

exercises.explain: Yes! Just like wiping fog gradually, the AI removes noise gradually over many steps to reveal a clear image.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'AI image generators are slow because they draw the picture pixel by pixel, like a printer.' Is this explanation correct, and why or why not?

options.text: No — they take many steps because each step only removes a small amount of noise, gradually revealing the whole image, not because they draw pixel by pixel
Yes — printers and AI generators work the exact same way, pixel by pixel
No — AI generators actually create the full image in one instant step
Yes — but only for black and white images, not color ones

exercises.explain: The correct idea is that sampling takes many steps because noise is removed gradually across the whole image each time, matching how it was gradually added during training — not because the AI is drawing pixel by pixel like a printer.
